page: Vultr voc-m-32c-256gb-1600s-amd is an instance within the Optimized Cloud Compute family, featuring an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD EPYC Milan processor. The server provides 32 physical cores and 32 vCPUs running at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz, utilizing a shared CPU allocation. It is equipped with 256.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 8.0 GB of RAM per core. Storage is bundled as a 1600 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

In benchmark testing, the server demonstrates strong compute capabilities despite its shared CPU allocation. The stress-ng single-core CPU performance benchmark recorded a score of 3113.46, placing it in the top tier of tested instances. Multi-core performance under stress-ng reached 99821.78, which is categorized as strong. The processor supports instruction set extensions including AVX, AES, SHA_NI, BMI2, and FMA, which assist in cryptographic and mathematical operations.

The qualitative cost efficiency of this instance is defined by its high memory-to-vCPU ratio and integrated NVMe storage, offering a dense resource profile for memory-intensive workloads. However, the shared CPU allocation represents a potential performance tradeoff under sustained, concurrent multi-core utilization. This server configuration is suitable for memory-optimized databases, large-scale caching layers, data processing pipelines, and general-purpose enterprise applications requiring substantial memory capacity and fast local storage.
description: Vultr voc-m-32c-256gb-1600s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance built on the x86_64 architecture with an AMD EPYC Milan processor. It features 32 vCPUs and 32 physical cores running at 2.0 GHz under a shared CPU allocation model, paired with 256.0 GB of system memory and a bundled 1600 GB NVMe SSD. In benchmark evaluations, the server achieved a top-tier single-core stress-ng score of 3113.46 and a strong multi-core score of 99821.78. The 8.0 GB of memory per core and integrated NVMe storage provide a cost-efficient profile for memory-heavy applications, though the shared CPU allocation introduces potential performance variability under sustained workloads. This instance is suited for memory-optimized databases, caching layers, data processing pipelines, and general-purpose enterprise applications.
